name: Argument Structure: Transitive
intuition: The lexical verb encodes a two-participant event involving an external argument (agent/initiator) and an internal argument (patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the external argument toward and affecting the internal argument.
set_to_1_if: The target verb introduces an overt grammatical subject and an overt direct object.

The clause is morphologically passive, and the lexical verb licenses both an external argument (agent) and an internal argument (patient), even if the external argument is unexpressed or realized in a 'by'-phrase.
The verb licenses two internal arguments (ditransitive pattern), including constructions with both a direct and an indirect object.
set_to_0_if: The target verb does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object).
The verb is used without an overt internal argument in an activity reading (e.g., 'He has eaten', 'She has been reading'), where no affected patient is syntactically realized.
The target verb functions as a copula linking the subject to a predicative complement (e.g., 'be', 'become', 'seem'), rather than introducing an internal argument.
edge_cases: Ambitransitive verbs are marked 1 only when an overt internal argument is present (e.g., 'He has broken the vase'); in their intransitive alternation without an overt object (e.g., 'The vase has broken'), they are marked 0.
Reflexive constructions are marked 1 only when the reflexive pronoun is overt (e.g., 'He has seen himself'); objectless uses with an implied reflexive reading (e.g., 'He has shaved') are marked 0.
examples_positive: She has loved the world. (Overt object -> 1)
She has sent him the letter. (Ditransitive -> 1)
examples_negative: He has eaten. (Object is implied, but not overt -> 0)
The vase has broken. (Ambitransitive without external argument -> 0)
